explanation: Standard implant-to-IAN safety margin taught in implant surgery is about 2 mm apical clearance from the canal to reduce neuropraxia risk on placement.
Esthetic anterior single-tooth sites (e.g., #11) favor bone-level implants for subcrestal positioning, emergence profile control, and soft-tissue architecture versus tissue-level platforms.
End-stage renal disease produces uremic fetor from volatile nitrogenous compounds (ammonia/urea breakdown); xerostomia coexists but the classic breath odor mechanism is ammonia.
In posterior mandible, maintain roughly 2 mm between implant apex and IAN canal on CBCT planning to protect the nerve from heat and intrusion trauma.
Missing #11–13 is a single edentulous span crossing the midline with teeth remaining posteriorly—Kennedy Class IV (not III, which is a unilateral bounded posterior span).
Publishing identifiable face photos without consent violates patient privacy (and confidentiality of care). Among listed options, patient privacy is the direct breach.
Fiber posts are the easiest to retrieve: they can be drilled out with ultrasonics/special burs, unlike cast posts or tightly engaged metal screw posts that risk root fracture on removal.
A V-shaped cingulum rest seat on a canine is classically prepared with an inverted-cone bur to form the triangular outline and sharp line angles for rest support.
Silicate/zinc phosphosilicate-type cements historically leach fluoride; among given choices that is the fluoride-releasing option (true GICs also release F but are not listed).
In four-handed dentistry, sharps are passed in the neutral/transfer zone between operator and assistant, never across the patient’s face or into the working zone blindly.
Multi-surface carious primary molars are best restored with stainless steel crowns for full coverage, durability, and seal—superior to multi-surface amalgam/composite in high-risk pedo.
Patients with intellectual disability/Down syndrome who cannot reliably spit should not use fluoride mouthrinses (ingestion risk); varnish/paste/supplements under supervision are preferred.
Low caries-risk children typically need bitewings only every 12–24 months; a 6-month recall alone does not mandate new radiographs if prior films are recent and exam is stable.
Bonding orthodontic buttons during surgical canine exposure fails most often from blood/saliva contamination—moisture control under a flap is difficult and breaks the resin bond.
When residual mandibular ridges lie buccal to the maxillary ridge, posterior teeth are set in bilateral crossbite so forces stay over the ridges and denture bases remain stable.
Posting a patient’s clinical photos without authorization breaches confidentiality of protected health information (and privacy); confidentiality is the core professional duty named here.
Lateral cephalometrics primarily assess anteroposterior skeletal and dental relationships (SNA/SNB/ANB, vertical proportions), not true transverse width.
In two-stage implant surgery the submerged fixture is sealed with a cover screw under mucosa until second-stage uncovering; healing abutments are transmucosal, not buried.
Isolated amalgam ditching ≈0.5 mm without caries, fracture, or symptoms is observed; ditching alone is not an automatic indication to replace or patch with composite.
Multiple untreated caries and abscesses with failed care access constitute dental neglect (a form of child maltreatment), not sexual or physical abuse patterns alone.
A mesiodistal line of discomfort with a restoration and no separation of fragments indicates a crack (incomplete fracture), distinct from craze lines (enamel only) or VRF/split tooth.
About 4 mm interocclusal height is marginal for cement-retained crowns (often need ≥6–7 mm) but can allow screw-retained restorations with careful abutment selection.
Limited vertical restorative space (~4 mm) favors screw-retained implant crowns over cement-retained designs that demand more height for abutment, cement space, and ceramic.
For two implants in 14 mm MD: ~1.5 mm to each adjacent tooth + ≥3 mm between implants leaves ~4 mm diameter each—two 4 mm fixtures fit; wider pairs violate spacing rules.

When a Class V composite fails off during scaling with cold pain, long-term resolution of recession/root exposure often requires soft-tissue grafting; rebonding alone does not correct mucogingival deficit.
A retained primary canine (#53) with good crown/root ratio and a poor-prognosis impacted permanent canine can be retained as the functional unit; extraction of unrelated teeth is not indicated.
Nonworking-side (balancing) interferences are the most destructive occlusal contacts, delivering lateral stresses that overload teeth, implants, and TMJ on the opposite side.
Multiquadrant mixed radiopaque/radiolucent lesions are classic for florid cemento-osseous dysplasia, not solitary cementoma or osteoid osteoma.
Extensive untreated caries/abscesses plus overall medical neglect pattern equals dental neglect; management includes reporting per jurisdiction and urgent infection control.
Long-term RPD base pain with indirect retainer lift under load indicates residual ridge resorption and loss of tissue support—reline (or remake) restores mucosal support and rest function.
Acrylic complete dentures left dry overnight dehydrate and warp; they will not seat until rehydrated. Store in water when out of the mouth.
Conventional glass ionomer sets by acid–base reaction (polyalkenoic acid + fluoroaluminosilicate glass). Composites set by polymerization; RMGI/compomers are dual mechanisms.
Saucer-shaped circumferential bone loss around an implant is radiographic peri-implantitis (not mucositis alone, which lacks progressive bone loss).
Multi-unit screw-retained implant frameworks risk nonpassive fit; strain from a nonpassive superstructure can cause screw loosening, bone loss, or fracture—passivity is critical.
After two-stage placement, implant platforms are closed with cover screws and buried under the flap until second-stage exposure; healing abutments are used only after uncovering.
Implant planning for an anterior single tooth requires diagnostic wax-up and a surgical guide/template driven by the planned prosthesis, not just CBCT and casts alone.
Fracture involving cementum, dentin, and pulp defines a root fracture (horizontal/oblique). Complicated crown fracture is enamel–dentin–pulp without cementum as the defining plane.
When MIC/ICP is stable and the patient functions there, a single crown is fabricated in maximum intercuspation rather than forcing a new CO position without full rehabilitation.
Tie dental floss to the screwdriver/driver before intraoral use so an ingested or aspirated screw/driver can be retrieved—especially with limited opening.
Cleft lip/palate patients commonly develop Class III patterns from maxillary hypoplasia and transverse deficiency after surgical scarring and growth disturbance.
At age 15, skeletal growth is incomplete—implants and definitive FPDs are deferred. A resin-bonded (Maryland) bridge is conservative interim replacement for a missing lateral.
Class V restorations are placed with composite (or RMGI) without a matrix band in most cases; isolation and contouring are freehand or with cervical matrices as needed, not standard TO matrix.
Mild Class V with recession is often managed by restorative coverage alone; soft-tissue grafting is reserved when root coverage, KT width, or esthetics demand surgery beyond the filling.
When a patient refuses to choose, autonomy requires re-explaining risks, benefits, and alternatives of RCT/post/crown versus extraction/FPD so she can decide—not paternalistic choice alone.
White patches with poor hygiene and high-carb diet plus maternal similar history fit rampant caries/demineralization better than amelogenesis imperfecta (developmental enamel defect).
In mixed dentition, horizontal bitewings best detect posterior interproximal and occlusal caries depth; PA films supplement specific teeth, OPG is less sensitive for early proximal caries.
Glass ionomer cement sets by acid–base reaction between polyacid liquid and glass powder; amalgam is metallic set, composite is free-radical polymerization.
Surface bubbles on a stone cast usually come from pouring without vibration or trapping air against the impression surface; bulk voids more often reflect poor stone mixing.
Severely resorbed ridges are managed with monoplane/neutrocentric occlusion to minimize lateral forces and leverage on unstable bases; anatomic teeth increase shear.
A radiographically “clear” necrotic lateral without caries often has dens invaginatus allowing bacterial ingress to the pulp while the clinical crown looks intact.
Chewing hypersensitivity shortly after onlay delivery is most often hyperocclusion (high contact); adjust occlusion before attributing symptoms to cement chemistry alone.
Long-term failure of PFM fixed partial dentures commonly involves caries at abutment margins; connector fracture and debonding are less frequent with adequate design.
The interforaminal mandibular anterior region is remote from the IAN and maxillary sinus, making it the safest zone for implant placement among the listed sites.
Polysulfide elastomers have the longest working and setting times among common elastomeric impression materials; alginate and PVS set faster.
Alginate sticking and tearing between teeth often follows inadequate wetting of dry teeth/soft tissues; material locks into undercuts and tears on removal.
After endodontic access with minimal structural loss (e.g., conservative palatal access), a bonded composite restoration is adequate; posts/crowns are for more compromised teeth.
When only mobile spaced mandibular anteriors remain, a lingual plate major connector splints and stabilizes them better than a lingual bar that needs more lingual depth.
High-speed preparation without water coolant generates heat that can necrose pulp and injure dentin—pulpal damage is the primary risk of dry cutting multiple crowns.
Early fracture of a newly cemented inlay most often reflects inadequate preparation resistance/retention form or thickness, not solely cement technique alone.
If a drawing shows an occlusal/cingulum rest without a prepared rest seat, the error is absence of rest seat preparation—the rest will not seat positively or direct forces axially.
When extrusion, endodontics, and a crown are all needed, complete RCT first for a sealable canal and predictable root length, then orthodontic extrusion, then definitive prosthodontics.
An RPD replacing a single anterior (#11) still needs positive rest support—typically rests on both adjacent abutments (two rests) for vertical support and stability.
Before any definitive prosthesis after years of edentulism with plaque, calculus, and proximal caries, disease control starts with scaling, OHI, and caries management—not implant or FPD first.
Sensitivity from abrasion/caries at the CEJ is managed first with restorative coverage (RMGI/composite); grafting is adjunctive when recession coverage or KT is the primary goal.
Rolled peri-implant gingiva with good hygiene and no excess cement suggests overcontoured emergence—adjust crown contours rather than unnecessary explantation.
Acute wheezing and dyspnea after rubber dam placement points to latex (or dam powder) allergy; remove dam, support airway, and treat as allergic reaction.

Hybrid composites achieve high filler loading by weight (often ~75–80%) combining large and small particles; microfills are low-filler; macrofills are older high-filler but hybrids are the classic “high filler” teaching answer here.
Permanent deformation of a cast RPD framework under function means stresses exceeded the proportional/elastic limit of the alloy so plastic deformation remains.
Severe lingering cold pain two weeks after veneer cementation indicates irreversible pulpitis—RCT is indicated, not mere veneer replacement alone.
Esthetic complaints with poor hygiene, caries, and skeletal issues require sequenced disease control (OH + restorations) before ortho and then possible orthognathic surgery.
Ideal final impression materials give adequate working time for seating and border molding, then a relatively short set to reduce distortion from movement.
Multiple facial caries in primary anteriors with esthetic demand are restored with composite (often strip crowns); SSC is for more extensive multi-surface destruction.
A single missing #21 with teeth on both sides is a bounded edentulous space—Kennedy Class III. Class IV requires a single anterior span that crosses the midline.
Divergent multi-implant impressions are best made open-tray (pick-up) so copings remain in the impression and path-of-withdrawal distortion is minimized versus closed tray.
Complete dentures in skeletal Class III often use nonanatomic (0°) teeth to accommodate the cross-arch relationship and reduce lateral forces on resorbed ridges.
Osteoclasts resorb bone during remodeling; osteoblasts form bone. PDL-associated remodeling of alveolar bone in response to force is osteoclast-mediated on the pressure side.
Polyether is hydrophilic and can absorb water (dimensional change if stored wet); it is stiff, relatively short-setting, and not more dimensionally stable than addition silicone in all conditions.
Missing lower central with healthy abutments: resin-bonded bridge is definitive yet conservative (minimal prep) versus full-coverage FPD or removable interim options.
Age 15 with SIP and grossly MOD #36: save the tooth with RCT and full-coverage restoration (post/core + crown if structure demands); implant is inappropriate before growth completion.
Pressuring a parent from composite to amalgam until she yields overrides voluntary informed choice—violation of autonomy (and of valid informed consent process).
A white plaque-like extension from CEJ toward furcation on exam is most consistent with periodontal soft-tissue change/calculus-related disease, not hypercalcification of enamel.
Correcting interproximal one-wall (hemiseptal) bony architecture during resective surgery uses osseous recontouring/flattening to eliminate craters and create positive architecture.
Missing #45–46 with 14 mm MD and 5 mm interocclusal space allows two ~4 mm implants if spacing rules are met; extract the supraerupted antagonist only if it cannot be managed prosthetically.

On a Class II mandibular RPD, the indirect retainer is placed as far from the fulcrum line as practical—typically a rest on the opposite anterior abutment (e.g., premolar #44 depending on design drawing).
Financial or product ties to an implant company create a conflict of interest that must be disclosed; the ethical issue named is conflict of interest, not beneficence alone.
Pressure-indicating paste (zinc oxide–eugenol rouge with chloroform solvent historically, or modern PIP) marks intaglio contacts causing mental-area numbness from overextension/pressure.
Using an old complete denture as provisional for a new one is blocked if severe occlusal wear has destroyed VDO and occlusal plane—slight looseness alone can still allow use as a template.
Bilateral upper molar loss with controlled diabetes (HbA1c ~7.2) still allows implant consideration when medical control and bone are adequate; implants are not absolutely contraindicated.
A heavily restored tooth with suboptimal prior endodontics warrants endodontic evaluation/retreatment referral before deciding crown versus extraction if restorability remains possible.
Most conservative fixed option for a single missing premolar (no implant listed) is a resin-bonded bridge; ceramic full-coverage FPD is more destructive; interim RPD is not definitive.
Enlarged adenoids/nasopharyngeal obstruction can worsen gagging with dentures; DM and xerostomia affect mucosa and retention differently than primary gag reflex triggers.
High caries-risk adults may use prescription high-fluoride toothpaste (e.g., 2800–5000 ppm F) under guidance; ~1450 ppm is OTC standard, 500 ppm is too low for high risk.
Heavy calculus and periodontal disease must be controlled with scaling/root planing and hygiene before definitive crowns or continuing elective ortho on periodontally unstable teeth.
Classic periodontology teaching: osseous craters are a large fraction of defects (~35% overall) and predominate in the mandible (~60%+ of craters)—match the 35%/62% style figures.

Anterior implant platforms are placed slightly lateral to the midline of the planned tooth, not into the incisive canal or dead center of the papilla, to protect nasopalatine structures and emergence.
Skeletal Class III complete dentures typically use 0° monoplane teeth to manage the reverse horizontal overlap and reduce lateral forces on unstable bases.
Full-mouth rehabilitation after removing defective restorations is organized in centric relation (a repeatable reference), not habitual MIC alone, when reorganizing occlusion.
PVS is excellent for relatively parallel implants; stiffer polyether can help with nonparallel multi-implant impressions, though open-tray technique matters more than brand alone.
Closed-mouth (functional) impressions are used for resorbed ridges needing border molding under function; flabby ridges need selective pressure/mucostatic open-tray approaches.
Dull pain with percussion/palpation findings pointing to a large carious #37 approaching the pulp is most likely the source; a superficial #36 composite and already-treated #35 are less probable.
A provisional crown on a prepared peg lateral primarily protects the pulp and prepared dentin while tissues settle; esthetics and OH are secondary benefits of coverage.
Industry collaboration that biases implant choice without disclosure is a conflict of interest requiring transparency and patient-centered product selection.
Facets and high shiny amalgams with bruxism signs are managed by occlusal adjustment and a night guard, not wholesale amalgam replacement for mythical toxicity.
Immediate implant at age ~13 then disharmony at 17 reflects ongoing skeletal/alveolar growth around a fixed implant—implants should wait until growth is complete.
Cephalometric radiographs yield anteroposterior and vertical skeletal relationships; transverse diagnosis needs PA ceph, CBCT, or models, not lateral ceph alone.
Xylitol chewing gum reduces caries mainly by suppressing Streptococcus mutans and reducing acidogenic plaque metabolism, not by changing enamel crystal chemistry directly.
Sudden pain during shallow enamel preparation without anesthesia is classically from odontoblastic processes in enamel spindles transmitting stimuli toward the pulp.
After removing old PFMs with gingivitis, disease control (hygiene, debridement, caries control) precedes impressions and new crowns—never prosthetics on inflamed tissues first.
Crown lengthening with only 2 mm KT and 3 mm bone sounding (supracrestal tissue attachment needs ~3 mm) requires apically positioned flap with osseous reduction, not gingivectomy alone.
Replacing a central incisor crown must respect anterior guidance (incisal/canine paths) so protrusive and lateral contacts are harmonious and posterior disclusion is preserved.
Enamel–dentin fracture without pulp exposure is an uncomplicated crown fracture; complicated fractures involve the pulp and need different pulpal management.
McGill/York consensus: the standard of care for mandibular edentulism is a two-implant overdenture, not conventional CD alone when implants are feasible.
The submerged cover screw seals the implant during submerged healing; healing abutments shape soft tissue after second-stage uncovering.
With limited KT and bone sounding indicating biologic width violation risk, crown lengthening needs osseous recontouring plus apical flap positioning, not soft tissue excision alone.
NUG with poor hygiene: first-line is gentle debridement (scaling) and irrigation/OHI; elective third-molar extraction and extensive restorations wait until acute infection is controlled.
Crowns that match under one light but mismatch under another demonstrate metamerism—different spectral reflectance relative to natural tooth under changed illuminants.
Increasing sensitivity months after amalgam, if progressive and cold-lingering, suggests irreversible pulpitis from residual caries, microleakage, or preparation trauma—evaluate pulp status.
Contemporary survival of single-tooth implant crowns is typically reported above 95% at medium-term follow-up in healthy patients with proper planning.
Fractured cusp with severe bite pain often implies pulp involvement or need for full cuspal coverage after endodontic assessment—RCT with post/core and crown when structure and pulp demand it.
Community water fluoridation remains the most effective population-level caries preventive measure versus individual topical fluoride or diet counseling alone.
Bruxism with missing posteriors overloads anterior PFMs—porcelain chipping is from occlusal overload and loss of posterior support, not only thin porcelain.
Ill-fitting CD with nodular epulis fissuratum needs excision of the hyperplastic tissue (after tissue rest/conditioning); reline alone on hyperplastic tissue fails.
Ante’s law for replacing #24–25: abutment root surface area of #23 and #26 should equal or exceed pontics—canine (#23) plus first molar (#26) typically suffice without extra molars.
Among hybrid, macrofill, microfill, and flowable, hybrid composites are formulated for high filler weight percent with good strength; microfills and flowables are lower filler.
Persistent numbness after upper left MOD work suggests anesthetic trauma or injection near greater palatine/posterior superior alveolar distribution rather than true allergy one day later.

Temperature sensitivity with wear facets, long buccal cusp, and increased mobility after gold onlay indicates occlusal trauma/high contacts; polish/adjust occlusion—options omit “high occlusion,” so address premature contact/polish-related high spots.
Marginal ditching of ~0.5 mm without secondary caries or structural failure is monitored clinically/radiographically; automatic replacement is not required.
The posterior palatal seal is scraped into the maxillary cast with a Kingsley scraper (or similar) after functional border molding, not with a surgical blade as first choice.
Posting a child’s clinical selfie without consent violates privacy (and confidentiality); privacy is correct when confidentiality is not listed separately as stronger.
Kennedy Class I RPD without an indirect retainer that shows excessive movement needs design repair (add indirect retainer/rests), not only reline of the base.
Unilateral painless maxillary swelling with ground-glass radiograph is classic fibrous dysplasia of bone.
Publicizing a famous patient’s treatment photos without authorization breaches confidentiality of the doctor–patient relationship and PHI.
Modified ridge-lap pontics are common in maxillary FPDs for esthetics with cleansable tissue contact; ovate needs surgical site preparation; conical is more mandibular posterior.
Flabby ridges need open-tray selective-pressure or mucostatic impressions so displaceable tissue is not compressed into a distorted cast.
Implant diameters roughly 3.5–4.1 mm are regular/standard platform; mini implants are smaller (~≤3 mm); wide platforms are ≥5 mm.
Posting identifiable patient cases without consent violates confidentiality; privacy and consent issues coexist, but confidentiality is the standard professional answer.
House classification: an exacting patient is demanding, brings multiple prior dentures, and dictates precise details—philosophical patients are cooperative and trusting.
Discomfort with a visible mesiodistal line under MOD amalgam without complete separation indicates a crack tooth pattern, not yet a split tooth or simple craze line.
Fear of amalgam “toxicity” requires education that intact amalgams need not be replaced for systemic toxicity, then offer referral if the patient still insists on elective replacement.
Kennedy Class III is tooth-supported; indirect retainers are not required (no distal-extension fulcrum rotation)—zero indirect retainers.
Minimum interocclusal space often cited for screw-retained implant crowns is about 4–5 mm from implant platform to opposing dentition; cement-retained needs more.
Addition silicone (PVS) is dimensionally stable and can be poured multiple times; alginate is single-pour and unstable; polysulfide is less ideal for delayed multiple pours.
Most common CEJ relationship is cementum overlapping enamel (~60%); edge-to-edge and enamel-over-cementum are less frequent; gaps expose dentin.
Strip crowns are indicated for multi-surface/facial caries of primary anteriors needing full esthetic coverage with composite shells.
An 18-year-old adult consents for himself; parental consent is not required once the patient is a legal adult (jurisdiction norms for majority age).
Maxillary central incisors: distal incisal angle is more rounded than the sharper mesial angle—use crown outline to orient #11 vs #21 before replantation.
Coronal-third root fractures have the poorest prognosis (mobility, periodontal communication); apical fractures have better prognosis; midroot is intermediate.
Anteroposterior rotational movement of a distal-extension RPD after delivery indicates missing or ineffective indirect retention along the fulcrum line.
Post failure is classically linked to short and wide posts that weaken the root and lack ferrule/resistance; adequate length and conservative diameter improve survival.

Mandibular denture should cover most of the retromolar pad (commonly taught ~2/3 to full pad). Covering only 1/3 is underextended; among options, half-pad wording is the underextension teaching answer.
Narrow deep isolated buccal pocket on a multiyear post-crown tooth with bite pain suggests vertical root fracture—extraction is usually required, not retreatment.
Standard of care for the edentulous mandible is a two-implant overdenture; four implants is optimal fixed/removable support but not the minimum standard.
If pour is delayed ~24 h, choose dimensionally stable PVS (addition silicone); hydrocolloids distort rapidly; polyether can absorb water if poorly stored.
Mild cold discomfort 1–2 days after moderate-depth composite with normal occlusion and radiographs is common dentinal hypersensitivity from polymerization stress/open tubules—monitor.
Cavity varnish under amalgam seals dentinal tubules, reducing microleakage and chemical irritation—pulp protection function (also limits metal ion discoloration of dentin).
With strong gag reflex limiting isolation/etching time, glass-ionomer sealant is moisture-tolerant and fluoride-releasing versus resin sealants needing dry field.
New CD gagging while eating often comes from excessive bulk of posterior borders/palate thickness rather than under-extension of the PPS alone.
Dysphagia and difficulty swallowing after new complete dentures commonly reflect excessive vertical dimension of occlusion reducing freeway space.

Epinephrine-impregnated retraction cord is avoided or used cautiously in patients on ACE inhibitors (e.g., lisinopril) and other cardiovascular risks; prefer aluminum chloride/ferric sulfate alternatives as indicated.
Single missing anterior tooth with remaining teeth posteriorly is Kennedy Class III (bounded). Class IV crosses the midline as one anterior edentulous area.
Only 1 mm freeway space means excessive VDO—reduce occlusal height (lab remount/grind teeth) rather than reassure or grind intaglio alone for swallowing difficulty and sore mucosa.
Apprehensive pedo with multi-surface primary molar caries: stainless steel crown is the durable full-coverage choice over multi-surface amalgam/composite.

Platelets 70k and WBC 1500 with severe pain: if absolute neutrophils allow and platelets are acceptable for simple extraction with local measures, give antibiotic coverage and treat urgent infection rather than endless deferral.
Anaphylaxis (urticaria, lip swelling, dyspnea): supine if hypotensive, oxygen, monitor, and intramuscular epinephrine immediately while awaiting EMS—not delayed IV-only plans in the dental chair.
Four millimeters of interocclusal space after implant impression favors screw-retained prosthetics; cement-retained needs greater vertical room for abutment and ceramic bulk.
Anterior slide from CR to MIC is adjusted on mesial inclines of maxillary (and distal of mandibular) posterior teeth—BULL/LUBL rules guide selective grinding of premature contacts.
Full-mouth fixed rehabilitation in dentate patients is commonly organized with mutually protected occlusion and canine guidance when canines are restorable and periodontally sound.
If the retentive arm engages before the reciprocal arm seats, the abutment is torqued during insertion—mobility can result; reciprocal arm should contact as retention engages.
RPD stability against horizontal/vertical seating forces is provided primarily by rests and rigid minor connectors (with bracing arms); retention alone does not stabilize.
Final complete-denture impressions commonly use PVS (addition silicone) for accuracy and stability; alginate is for preliminaries; agar is less used clinically today.
When multiple primary teeth are missing but permanent molars and incisors have erupted, a lingual arch is the bilateral fixed space maintainer of choice.
Oroantral communication prophylaxis/treatment typically uses amoxicillin–clavulanate for mixed oral/sinus flora; plain penicillin alone is less broad for sinus pathogens.
Extensive facial-to-palatal primary anterior caries with esthetic demand: SSC with open-face/resin facing or strip-crown composite hybrid solutions; plain GIC is less esthetic/durable.
Five mm from restoration margin to bone with 2 mm from finish line to bone and 2 mm axial wall indicates adequate biologic width (~2 mm) and sufficient ferrule—no crown lengthening required.

Very deep caries stimulating odontoblasts after pulp injury deposits reparative (tertiary) dentin at the pulp–dentin interface; reactionary dentin forms with milder stimuli from surviving odontoblasts.
Class V lesions, especially root/cervical, are often best with RMGI for fluoride, chemical adhesion to dentin, and moisture tolerance; pure composite needs excellent isolation.
A movable gold crown with normal probing and bite discomfort warrants removal and inspection for cracked tooth or lost retention; simply reseating without diagnosis risks missing fracture.
Maryland (resin-bonded) metal wings gain micromechanical retention from air-particle abrasion with alumina (~50 µm), not hydrofluoric acid (used for glass ceramics).

Extracted teeth without amalgam are regulated as contaminated waste/regulated medical waste categories; amalgam-containing teeth are hazardous for mercury—contaminated is the general classification asked.
Facial asymmetry is evaluated with posteroanterior (PA) cephalometric radiograph (and clinical exam); lateral ceph is primarily AP/vertical, not transverse asymmetry.
A periodontal probe is useful to detect and remove excess cement around implant restorations as well as for pocket depth and BOP assessment.
Fractured metal overdenture coping on a restorable root with a satisfactory denture: remake support with fiber post and composite build-up rather than recementing a broken metal core.
Lingual bar trauma is avoided by proper superior border placement (≥3–4 mm from free gingival margin) and relief—design and relief protect soft tissue.
Large palatal torus with anterior missing teeth often needs an anteroposterior palatal strap design that avoids the torus rather than a full plate over the bony mass.
Metal trays are cleaned then chemically disinfected (e.g., glutaraldehyde immersion per protocol) before autoclaving; organic debris must be removed first.
Bilateral parasymphyseal fractures can collapse the tongue base and obstruct the airway—airway assessment and protection come before occlusion or TMJ checks (ATLS priorities).
Extracted teeth for preclinic bonding research are commonly stored in distilled water (sometimes with thymol); formalin fixes tissue but can alter substrate properties for bonding studies.
Only #13, #14, #24 remain: bilateral distal-extension edentulous areas with an additional modification space → Kennedy Class I modification 1.
Inability to close the eye after IANB/injection suggests facial nerve (VII) anesthesia from lateral/posterior needle deviation into the parotid—lateral displacement is classic teaching.
Paroxysmal electric facial pain triggered by light touch with a post-ictal refractory period is classic trigeminal neuralgia, not atypical odontalgia.
Urticaria, rhinorrhea, and conjunctivitis after a dental visit with dam/gloves points to type I latex allergy (immediate hypersensitivity), not delayed amalgam type IV from 3-year-old fillings.
Down syndrome patients who cannot reliably expectorate should avoid fluoride mouthrinses (swallowing risk); varnish and supervised dentifrice remain appropriate.
Mesial caries on a maxillary primary/permanent incisor is anesthetized via anterior superior alveolar infiltration/block (or local infiltration), not PSA or nasopalatine alone.
Low frenum with diastema: the blanch test (pull upper lip—papilla blanches) helps decide frenectomy need after assessing space and canine eruption status.
When combining complete and partial dentures, lingualized occlusion is often chosen for stability and ease of balancing contacts across the CD–RPD combination.
Kennedy Class I with infrabulge clasp contraindicated and non-cervical undercut: use a suprabulge design—mesial rest with distal circumferential clasp (RPA-type) rather than any I-bar.
Macrofilled composites have high filler content by weight; microfills have low filler (~35–50%) for polish; nanofills vary but the classic contrast is macrofill high vs microfill low.
Well-controlled diabetes with radiographic bone loss: removable prosthesis is safer than implants on deficient bone without grafting/assessment; fixed may overload periodontally reduced abutments.
MOD amalgam with bite pain and normal radiograph suggests cracked tooth (incomplete fracture), not craze lines (asymptomatic enamel) or proven VRF without other signs.
Covering only one-third of the retromolar pad is underextended; mandibular CD should cover most of the pad (commonly taught ~two-thirds to entire pad) for support and border seal.
